title: The On-Cost Gap: Why Recruitment and Labour Hire Margins Compress Every July 1
linkedin:
The Fair Work wage rise landed on 1 July. For most SMEs it's one line on the P&L. For recruitment and labour hire businesses, it's the direct input to almost every dollar of revenue, and it moves differently.

Here's the pattern we see every year in this industry.

Super, payroll tax, workers comp and leave loading are all calculated as a percentage of the base pay rate. When that rate steps up, every one of those on-costs steps up with it, from the very first payroll cycle. The total movement is larger than the headline award percentage suggests.

Client bill rates don't move at the same speed. Most sit inside contracts with their own review dates or a formal renegotiation process, sometimes months away from the day the cost actually changed.

That gap is where margin compresses, and it's rarely visible in the moment. Total revenue can keep climbing as placements and hours grow, while margin per placement underneath that growth is already thinner than it was in June. The variance usually only shows up once the monthly result closes, weeks after the cost has already been absorbed.

The businesses that hold their margin through this window are the ones who reviewed bill rates against the new cost base before 1 July, not the ones waiting for the P&L to confirm the damage.

If margin per placement isn't a number on your desk weekly right now, this is the month to start.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

#SMEFinance #ProfitabilityAdvice #AustralianBusiness
facebook:
A note for anyone running a recruitment or labour hire business. The wage rise that landed on 1 July doesn't just lift what you pay placed workers, it lifts super, payroll tax, workers comp and leave loading at the same time, because all of them are calculated off that base rate. Client bill rates, meanwhile, are usually locked into contracts that won't move for weeks or months.

That gap between cost moving now and revenue catching up later is where margin quietly disappears every year, often while total revenue keeps climbing and the change stays invisible until the month closes.

Have you already checked your bill rates against the new cost base, or is that still sitting on the August to-do list?

#SMEFinance
google:
A note for recruitment and labour hire business owners across Brisbane, Queensland and the wider east coast this July.

The Fair Work Commission's annual wage decision took effect from the first full pay period of the month, and it behaves differently in this industry than in most others. The pay rate you charge for isn't just a cost line, it's the direct input to almost every dollar of revenue. When that base rate rises, superannuation, payroll tax, workers compensation and leave loading all rise with it immediately, because each is calculated as a percentage of the same base.

Client bill rates rarely move at the same pace. Most sit inside contracts with their own review dates or a formal renegotiation process, which can leave weeks or months where the cost has already changed and the rate charged to the client has not.

That gap is where margin gets quietly given away every July, often while total revenue keeps growing and the change stays invisible until the monthly numbers are closed.

If your bill rates haven't been checked against this year's new cost base yet, now is the time. Read the full piece on the ProfitPulse blog for how to approach the review.
